turns.text: i want to write literature review about various comorbidities  in spinal cord injury?

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
Introduction:

Spinal cord injury (SCI) is a catastrophic condition that results in a loss of motor, sensory and autonomic function in various parts of the body. Along with the psychological and social impacts of this condition, individuals with SCI are also at an increased risk of developing various comorbidities. Comorbidities are medical conditions that occur alongside an underlying primary disease. SCI comorbidities can range from respiratory, cardiac, gastrointestinal, to psychological and other neurologic disorders. Furthermore, the presence of comorbidities can significantly impact the health outcomes and quality of life of individuals with SCI. This literature review aims to identify and evaluate research on the various comorbidities associated with SCI.

Respiratory comorbidities:

Respiratory complications are the leading cause of morbidity and mortality among individuals with SCI. The risk of respiratory infections, pneumonia, and respiratory failure increases significantly in this population due to the loss of muscle function in the diaphragm and intercostal muscles, leading to reduced lung compliance and increased susceptibility to infections. In a systematic review conducted by Berlowitz et al. (2013), it was found that respiratory complications are associated with a higher mortality rate in individuals with SCI. The study also showed that the risk of respiratory complications is greater in individuals with high-level injuries.

Cardiovascular comorbidities:

Individuals with SCI are at increased risk of developing cardiovascular disease (CVD) due to reduced physical activity, loss of muscle mass and strength, and autonomic dysfunction. A meta-analysis conducted by West et al. (2018) showed that individuals with SCI have a higher prevalence of hypertension, dyslipidemia, and metabolic syndrome than the general population. Another study conducted by Biering-Sorensen et al. (2017) reported that the risk of CVD is significantly higher in individuals with SCI compared to the general population, and the risk is further increased in those with high-level injuries.

Gastrointestinal comorbidities:

Gastrointestinal (GI) problems are common among individuals with SCI due to impaired bowel and bladder function, reduced mobility and physical activity, and altered autonomic regulation. GI complications that occur frequently in individuals with SCI include constipation, fecal incontinence, and gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). In a systematic review and meta-analysis conducted by Krogh et al. (2017), it was found that the prevalence of constipation and fecal incontinence is high among individuals with SCI, and the risk increases with increasing severity of the injury. The study also showed that the prevalence of GERD is higher in individuals with SCI compared to the general population.

Psychological comorbidities:

The psychological impact of SCI can be significant, with many individuals experiencing depression, anxiety, and posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD). The incidence of these psychological comorbidities is higher in individuals with SCI compared to the general population. A systematic review conducted by Craig et al. (2015) found that up to 50% of individuals with SCI experience depression at some point in their lives, and up to 56% experience anxiety. Furthermore, a study conducted by Kennedy et al. (2016) reported that the prevalence of PTSD is higher in individuals with SCI compared to those with other traumatic injuries.

Conclusion:

In conclusion, SCI is associated with a range of comorbidities that can significantly impact the health outcomes and quality of life of affected individuals. Respiratory, cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, and psychological comorbidities are among the most common comorbidities in SCI and should be actively monitored and treated. Clinicians and researchers should work together to develop comprehensive approaches to manage the comorbidities associated with SCI and improve the overall health and well-being of affected individuals.

turns.text: i want to write systematic literature review about various comorbidities in spinal cord injury in iran ?what is the best step?

turns.speaker: assistant
turns.text:
The following steps can be helpful in conducting a systematic literature review of comorbidities in spinal cord injury in Iran:

1. Define the research question: Define the research question and the objectives of the review. For instance, the research question could be "What are the prevalent comorbidities in individuals with spinal cord injury in Iran?"

2. Search strategy: Develop an appropriate search strategy to identify relevant studies that have investigated the comorbidities associated with spinal cord injury in Iran. This may involve searching multiple databases, such as PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science, along with local Iranian databases.

3. Study selection: Screen the identified studies based on their relevance to the research question and inclusion/exclusion criteria. This step will involve reviewing the titles, abstracts, and full texts of the studies.

4. Data extraction: Extract relevant data from the included studies, such as the study design, sample size, participant characteristics, comorbidities investigated, and prevalence rates of comorbidities.

5. Data synthesis: Synthesize the findings of the included studies using appropriate statistical methods, such as meta-analysis, if appropriate. This step will involve summarizing the prevalence rates of the various comorbidities in individuals with spinal cord injury in Iran.

6. Risk of bias assessment: Assess the quality of the included studies, and evaluate the risk of bias in the study design and methodology. This step will help to determine the strength of the evidence base and the validity of the conclusions drawn from the review.

7. Reporting: Report the findings of the systematic literature review in a clear and concise manner, adhering to the standards for systematic reviews outlined in the PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) guidelines. 

Overall, conducting a systematic literature review of comorbidities in spinal cord injury in Iran will involve comprehensively searching the relevant literature, evaluating the quality of the identified studies, and synthesizing the results to provide sound recommendations for future research and clinical practice.
